answer: The three modalities are: (1) the classical approach (complete enumeration in a single operation), (2) the modular approach (core module plus sample-based supplementary thematic modules), and (3) the use of registers in combination with fieldwork data collection.
excerpt: For the purposes of this publication, three modalities are discussed, including the two basic methodological approaches: the classical approach and the modular approach, the latter introduced in the WCA 2010. The third modality involves the use of registers in combination with fieldwork data collection. The distinguishing features lie in the design of the three modalities, rather than in whether complete enumeration is combined with sample enumeration.

question: What is the classical approach to conducting an agricultural census?
answer: The classical approach is a conventional, one-off operation in which all items — at minimum all essential items — are collected from all agricultural holdings using a single questionnaire (or a short and long questionnaire combination) through complete enumeration.
excerpt: In a few instances, countries wishing to add items that are not usually covered in the census, resort to a combination of a short and a long questionnaire to reduce costs and burden on the respondents. In this case, the short questionnaire should contain all the essential items recommended by the WCA 2030 and relevant items needed to build the frames for the programme of agricultural surveys. Both the short and long questionnaires are completed during the same field visit and use the same reference period.

question: What is the modular approach to conducting an agricultural census?
answer: The modular approach consists of a clearly defined core module covering all essential items by complete enumeration, and one or more sample-based supplementary thematic modules that collect additional items from subsets of holdings identified through the core module frame.
excerpt: The modular approach consists of a clearly defined core module and one or more supplementary thematic modules, with the items collected in the core module used to construct the frame for the sample-based supplementary modules. This approach is suitable for countries that do not have a well-established agricultural survey system.

question: What is the difference between the classical approach and the modular approach?
answer: In the classical approach all items are collected in a single field visit from all holdings. In the modular approach the core module collects all essential items by complete enumeration, while additional items are collected in separate sample-based supplementary thematic modules during a subsequent visit.
excerpt: The long questionnaire should not be confused with a supplementary module of the modular approach, which is thematic in nature and implemented in a separate field visit following the core module. In the modular approach, the core module includes the relevant items needed to build the frame for the subsequent sample-based supplementary thematic modules, as well as all essential items recommended at the lowest administrative level.

question: What is the core module in the modular approach?
answer: The core module includes all essential items required at the lowest administrative level and all frame-building items for supplementary modules. It is administered to all agricultural holdings through complete enumeration.
excerpt: In the modular approach, the core module includes the relevant items needed to build the frame for the subsequent sample-based supplementary thematic modules, as well as all essential items recommended at the lowest administrative level. These items are necessary to capture data on rare events, provide benchmark data for current agricultural statistics and establish any thresholds. Accordingly, all essential items and the relevant frame-building items should be collected from all agricultural holdings in the core module.

question: What are supplementary thematic modules and how are they used?
answer: Supplementary thematic modules are sample-based census components covering specific themes, administered to subsets of holdings identified through the core module. They collect additional items and may be conducted during or after the core module fieldwork.
excerpt: The sample-based supplementary thematic modules use the frame generated from the core module to target specific populations. These may include all holdings, holdings above a certain size or subsets of agricultural holdings, such as livestock or crop producers, with or without size thresholds. Supplementary modules are used to collect a broader range of data than in the classical approach.

question: What is the short-and-long questionnaire concept under the classical approach?
answer: Under the classical approach, countries may use a short questionnaire containing all essential items for all holdings and a long questionnaire containing additional items for a sample of holdings, both administered during the same field visit.
excerpt: In a few instances, countries wishing to add items that are not usually covered in the census, resort to a combination of a short and a long questionnaire to reduce costs and burden on the respondents. In this case, the short questionnaire should contain all the essential items recommended by the WCA 2030 and relevant items needed to build the frames for the programme of agricultural surveys. The long questionnaire should encompass additional items to be collected from a sample of holdings.

question: How can administrative records and registers be used in an agricultural census?
answer: Registers and administrative sources can supply some census data items, especially for the non-household sector, reducing the need for field visits. A unique identifier is essential to link data across sources. Registers are best combined with fieldwork since they rarely cover all essential items.
section_title: Use of registers and administrative records as a source of census data
excerpt: In most cases, registers cannot provide all the essential items, therefore the most reasonable approach is to use registers in combination with fieldwork data collection. Regardless of the census methodology adopted, it is extremely important that a unique primary key variable or identifier is used in all the data sources. The use of a unique identifier is essential in order to link information successfully.

question: What are the challenges of using administrative records in an agricultural census?
answer: Challenges include: restricted access to datasets; difficulties establishing cooperation with register owners; high access costs; incoherence in concepts, definitions and reference periods; and technical problems in linking data across sources.
section_title: Use of registers and administrative records as a source of census data
excerpt: The use of administrative records may pose some challenges, including: access to datasets is difficult or even impossible if the legal background is inadequate; it could be very difficult to establish a good cooperation with register owners; the cost for the access to the administrative records could be too high; incoherence of concepts, definitions, classification, and reference periods could hamper the use of administrative sources; and there could be technical problems related to linking data from various data sources.

question: What is the role of sampling frames in an agricultural census?
answer: A frame identifies all agricultural holdings to be enumerated without omission or duplication. An ideal frame is a list of all holdings based on the adopted operational definition. Frames are typically built from population censuses, farm registers, enumeration area lists, or a combination of sources.
excerpt: In a statistical collection, the frame is the mechanism by which the statistical units to be enumerated are identified, in this case the agricultural holdings. An ideal frame is a list of all agricultural holdings, based on the operational definition adopted by the country, that identifies each unit without omission or duplication and excludes any non-agricultural units.

question: What is a flexible enumeration strategy for agricultural censuses?
answer: Flexible enumeration strategies combine complete and sample enumeration to manage the logistical and financial demands of full coverage, while maintaining the integrity of census results. They allow countries to tailor census design to national agricultural structures and available resources.
section_title: Flexible enumeration strategies for agricultural censuses
excerpt: To address these constraints while maintaining the integrity and usefulness of census results, the WCA 2030 encourages flexible enumeration strategies that combine complete and sample enumeration in a complementary manner. These strategies allow countries to tailor their census design to national agricultural structures and available resources, ensuring cost-effectiveness while preserving data quality.

question: What is quality assurance in the context of an agricultural census?
answer: Quality assurance is the process of ensuring that quality goals are consistently met throughout the census data collection and processing system. It recommends that the accuracy of data collected be evaluated and reported so that users are informed of data limitations.
excerpt: Quality assurance is the process of ensuring that quality goals are consistently met throughout the entire system of census data collection and processing. It is therefore recommended that the accuracy of the data collected be evaluated and reported, so that census organizers are aware of quality levels and users are informed of data limitations.

question: What methods of enumeration are used in agricultural censuses?
answer: Common enumeration modes include pen-and-paper interviewing (PAPI), computer-assisted personal interviewing (CAPI), computer-assisted web interviewing (CAWI), and mail-out/mail-back. Technology including GPS, GIS, mobile devices and AI is increasingly used.
excerpt: The advent of advanced technologies, such as information technology, GIS and the global positioning system (GPS), has created new opportunities for data collection, with the potential to improve efficiency and data quality. By applying these technologies, countries can achieve gains in timeliness, reduce undercoverage and response errors, and lower costs by reducing staffing and other expenses. The traditional pen and paper mode is being replaced by mobile devices, online interviewing, and postal methods.

question: What is georeferencing and why is it important for the agricultural census?
answer: Georeferencing determines the geographic position of agricultural holdings using GPS or similar methods. It enables linkage of census data to GIS databases for spatial analysis, supports small area estimation and crop mapping, and helps reduce undercoverage.
section_title: Use of geospatial data to enhance census design and data collection
excerpt: An important factor in expanding the coverage of the census of agriculture is the georeferencing of agricultural holdings. GPS makes it possible to determine the geographic position of agricultural holdings, offering several advantages. Linking georeferenced agricultural census data with other GIS databases makes innovative analysis possible.

question: How can Earth Observation data enhance agricultural census statistics?
answer: Earth Observation data are used to produce land cover and land use maps, estimate holding area, develop wall-to-wall crop type maps, and support inter-censal crop area monitoring by integrating satellite imagery, classification algorithms and census field data.
section_title: Use of geospatial data to enhance census design and data collection
excerpt: Earth observation is the gathering of information about the Earth's surface, waters and atmosphere via ground-based, airborne and/or satellite remote sensing platforms. EO is increasingly used in agricultural surveys and censuses to produce land cover (LC) and land use (LU) statistics. Thanks to the integration of census and EO data, it is possible to set up a system to produce national, wall-to-wall crop type maps.

question: How is geospatial data used to estimate holding area?
answer: GIS software can estimate the area of any parcel or holding using GPS coordinates or digitised parcel boundaries. This can be combined with holder-declared areas and objective measurements from sample surveys to compute correction factors and improve area data accuracy.
section_title: Estimating holding area using georeferenced data
excerpt: It is possible to use a variety of proprietary and open-source GIS software to estimate the area of any parcel and of the holding itself, using any of the three approaches discussed above. The area calculation function embedded in the GIS software requires the user to define the geographic coordinate system used in the country and the area unit (e.g. ha). The accuracy of area estimation depends directly on the georeferencing approach used.

question: What are the essential items in the WCA 2030?
answer: The 27 essential items are the minimum data set all countries must collect by complete enumeration regardless of methodology. They include identification, land area, land use, land tenure, irrigation, crop areas, livestock numbers, fertilizer use, pesticide use, machinery use, work on the holding, and presence of aquaculture.
excerpt: There are 27 essential items. These represent the minimum data set that all countries should collect, regardless of the methodological approach used. They are critical for compiling a minimum set of national indicators on the agricultural sector, needed for policy-making and planning. Data for these items are required at small administrative unit level, such as districts or villages, or in the form of detailed cross-tabulations, and they are also needed for international comparison.

question: How many essential items are there in the WCA 2030?
answer: There are 27 essential items in the WCA 2030, up from 23 in the WCA 2020. The WCA 2030 has a total of 123 items: 27 essential and 96 additional.
excerpt: In the WCA 2030, there are a total of 123 items categorized as follows: 27 items are classified as essential (collected by complete enumeration), including those that serve for building frames for sample-based census supplementary modules and the continuous programme of surveys. 96 items are classified as additional (collected by sample enumeration). The WCA 2020 programme classified census items into three categories: essential items, frame items and additional items. In that programme, there were 23 essential items.

question: What are the additional items in the WCA 2030?
answer: The 96 additional items are preferably collected in supplementary modules, a long questionnaire or surveys — not by complete enumeration. They cover more detailed crop and livestock data, services, demographics, environmental practices and other topics for which small-area statistics are not critical.
excerpt: The remaining items are classified as additional and are preferably covered in surveys or in supplementary modules under the modular approach, or in the long questionnaire under the short–long questionnaire concept of the classical approach. In countries with integrated census and survey programmes, the agricultural census is used mainly to collect structural, slow-changing items, while surveys are used to collect fast-changing, non-structural additional items. The 96 items categorized as "additional" in the WCA 2030 are listed below under each theme.

question: What is the difference between essential and additional items?
answer: Essential items are the 27 structural items all countries must collect by complete enumeration for policy-making and international comparison. Additional items are the remaining 96 items, collected on a sample basis because detailed small-area statistics for them are not critical.
excerpt: There are 27 essential items. These represent the minimum data set that all countries should collect, regardless of the methodological approach used. The remaining items are classified as additional and are preferably covered in surveys or in supplementary modules under the modular approach, or in the long questionnaire under the short–long questionnaire concept of the classical approach.

question: What criteria determine whether an item should be included in the census?
answer: A decision tree guides the choice: if an item is one of the 27 essential items, it belongs in the census. If it is needed for building a frame, for small-area statistics, or for community-level data, it may also be included. Items that change quickly (reference period ≤ six months) belong in surveys, not the census.
excerpt: Is the item one of the WCA 2030's 27 essential items (e.g. holder sex, age and type, land use, crop areas and livestock numbers)? Is the item needed to build a frame for a census sample supplementary module or a follow-up survey? Is the item to be collected for national purposes to produce reliable small-area statistics, such as at the lowest geographical/administrative level (e.g. village) or for rare events? Does the item change quickly from year to year (e.g. quarterly/annually, such as crop and livestock production, agricultural prices, food consumption), and the reference period is <= six months?

question: What are the 12 themes of the WCA 2030 and which have essential items?
answer: There are 12 themes. Themes 1–6 and 9–10 have essential items. Themes 7 (Services for agriculture), 8 (Demographic and social characteristics), 11 (Forestry) and 12 (Fishing) have only additional items.
section_title: List of items for the census of agriculture
excerpt: THEME 7: SERVICES FOR AGRICULTURE — Additional items (see Theme 7 in Annex 4). THEME 8: DEMOGRAPHIC AND SOCIAL CHARACTERISTICS — Additional items (see Theme 8 in Annex 4). THEME 11: FORESTRY — Additional items (see Theme 11 in Annex 4). THEME 12: FISHING — Additional items (see Theme 12 in Annex 4).

question: Which themes have only additional items in the WCA 2030?
answer: Themes 7 (Services for agriculture), 8 (Demographic and social characteristics), 11 (Forestry) and 12 (Fishing) have only additional items — no essential items.
section_title: Theme 7 and Theme 8: Description of essential items
excerpt: THEME 7: SERVICES FOR AGRICULTURE — All items of this theme are classified as additional; see Annex 4. THEME 8: DEMOGRAPHIC AND SOCIAL CHARACTERISTICS — All items of this theme are classified as additional; see Annex 4.

question: What are community-level items in the WCA 2030?
answer: Community-level items are items collected at the community rather than holding level. They cover geography, socioeconomic conditions, community infrastructure and services, and development programmes. They are particularly relevant for decentralised planning and rural infrastructure policy.
excerpt: In addition to items collected at holding-level, the WCA 2030 also proposes items to be collected at community-level. These may be used to complement the data on holdings in the agricultural census. Community-level items are particularly relevant for decentralised planning, the design of targeted area development programmes and the assessment of infrastructure and services available to agricultural holdings.

question: What new essential items were introduced in the WCA 2030?
answer: Three items changed category from additional to essential in the WCA 2030: educational attainment of holder, spouse and manager (0107); selected machinery and equipment used on the holding by source (0604); and use of contractors for work on the holding (0905). Item 0402+ (area of temporary crops planted) is a completely new essential item.
excerpt: In the "Identification and general characteristics" theme, the item "Educational attainment of holder, spouse, and manager" (0107 in the WCA 2030) changed category from additional to essential item. In the "Agriculture practices" theme, the item "Selected machinery and equipment used on the holding by source" (0604) changed category from additional to essential item. Likewise, in the "Work on holding" theme, the item "Use of contractors for work on the holding according to type of service" (0905) changed category from additional to essential item.

question: What identification and location information is collected for agricultural holdings?
answer: Item 0101 collects the holding name, administrative unit code, holder's name, address and contact information, and the location of the holding. Location is normally identified through a geographic coding system based on the administrative structure of the country.
excerpt: Identification of the agricultural holding usually includes the holding name and administrative unit code, the holder's name, address and other contact information (telephone number, email address, etc.), and the location of the holding. Location is normally identified through a geographic coding system, based on the administrative structure of the country. Codes are provided for each administrative unit, such as province, district and/or village.

question: What is meant by the legal status of the agricultural holder?
answer: The legal status of the holder (type of holder) distinguishes between household-sector holdings (operated by household members) and non-household-sector holdings (corporations, cooperatives, tribes, clans, government institutions and other entities).
excerpt: The sector to which the holding belongs may be classified as "household sector" or "non-household sector". Countries are encouraged to distinguish between these two sectors in the census tabulation. Household sector holdings are those operated by household members. Non-household sector holdings are those that do not belong to the household sector. Corporations and cooperatives are defined within the framework of national laws and practices.

question: Why is data on the sex of the agricultural holder collected?
answer: Data on the sex of the agricultural holder are collected only for holdings in the household sector, to enable gender-disaggregated analysis and support monitoring of gender equality in agriculture, including SDG indicator 5.a.1.
excerpt: Item 0104 is important for analysing the gender aspects of agricultural production. Data on the sex of the agricultural holder are collected only for holdings with a civil person as the holder. Disaggregation of data by sex is a fundamental requirement for gender statistics.

question: What does main purpose of production mean for an agricultural holding?
answer: The main purpose of production indicates whether a holding produces primarily for sale or primarily for own consumption. The criterion is the activity that represents the larger value of agricultural production between own consumption and sale.
excerpt: The aim of this item is to obtain a broad indicator of the extent to which agricultural holdings participate in the market economy. In cases where a holding sells part of its produce and uses the rest for own consumption, the main purpose should be identified as the one that represents the larger value of agricultural production, either own consumption or sale. Sale includes selling produce for cash or in exchange for other produce (barter).

question: How is the total area of an agricultural holding defined?
answer: The total area of the holding is the area of all land making up the agricultural holding, including all land operated regardless of ownership. Land owned by household members but rented to others is excluded; land rented from others for agricultural production is included.
excerpt: The total area of the holding is the area of all land making up the agricultural holding. It provides a measure of holding size, which is an important element of agricultural census analysis. It includes all land operated by the holding, regardless of ownership title or legal form. Land owned by household members but rented to others should not be included in the holding area, while land not owned by household members but rented from others for agricultural production purposes should be included.

question: What is the FAO land use classification used in the agricultural census?
answer: The FAO land use classification is the internationally accepted standard for land use in the WCA 2030. For the agricultural census, nine basic land use classes are identified, covering all land within a holding from temporary crops to forest and aquaculture areas.
excerpt: The internationally accepted standard for land use is the FAO land use classification. The FAO land use classes are designed to cover the whole territory of a country. For the purposes of the agricultural census, it is recommended that nine basic land use classes be identified.

question: What are the nine land use classes in the WCA 2030?
answer: The nine basic land use classes are: (1) Land under temporary crops; (2) Land under temporary meadows and pastures; (3) Land temporarily fallow; (4) Land under permanent crops; (5) Land under permanent meadows and pastures; (6) Land under farm buildings and farmyards; (7) Forest and other wooded land; (8) Area used for aquaculture; (9) Other area not elsewhere classified.
excerpt: For the purposes of the agricultural census, it is recommended that nine basic land use classes be identified: Land under temporary crops; Land under temporary meadows and pastures; Land temporarily fallow; Land under permanent crops; Land under permanent meadows and pastures; Land under farm buildings and farmyards; Forest and other wooded land; Area used for aquaculture (including inland and coastal waters if part of the holding); Other area not elsewhere classified.

question: What is the difference between land use and land cover?
answer: Land use describes the purpose or activity for which land is used (e.g. crop production, pasture). Land cover describes the physical characteristics of the land surface (e.g. vegetation, soil, water). The two are related but distinct; land use is what matters for the agricultural census.
excerpt: The internationally accepted standard for land use is the FAO land use classification. Land use should be distinguished from "land cover", which describes the physical characteristics of the surface of the land. The two are related but distinct; land use is what matters for the agricultural census.

question: What is land under temporary crops?
answer: Land under temporary crops includes all land used for crops with a less than one-year growing cycle that must be newly sown or planted for further production after each harvest. Multiple or successive cropped areas are counted only once.
excerpt: Land under temporary crops includes all land used for crops with a less than one-year growing cycle which must be newly sown or planted for further production after the harvest. Some crops that remain in the field for more than one year may also be considered temporary crops, e.g. asparagus and sugar cane. Multiple/successive cropped areas are counted only once.

question: What is land temporarily fallow?
answer: Land temporarily fallow refers to land that is not seeded for one or more growing seasons but is expected to be returned to cultivation. Land left fallow for too long may be reclassified under another land use category.
excerpt: Land temporarily fallow refers to land that is not seeded for one or more growing seasons but is expected to be returned to cultivation. Land left fallow for too long may acquire characteristics that require it to be reclassified under another land use category.

question: What is land under permanent crops?
answer: Land under permanent crops refers to land cultivated with long-term crops that do not need to be replanted for several years after each harvest, such as coffee, cocoa, rubber and fruit trees. The ICC in Annex 6 specifies which crops are permanent.
excerpt: Land under permanent crops refers to land cultivated with long-term crops that do not need to be replanted for several years after each harvest. The ICC is provided in Annex 6, with the specification of permanent crops.

question: What is land under permanent meadows and pastures?
answer: Land under permanent meadows and pastures includes land used permanently (growth cycle of five years or more) to grow herbaceous forage crops, through cultivation or naturally, and that is used for grazing or hay production.
excerpt: Land under permanent meadows and pastures includes land used permanently (growth cycle of five years or more) to grow herbaceous forage crops, through cultivation or naturally (as wild prairie or grazing land). Permanent meadows and pastures on which trees and shrubs are grown should be recorded under this heading only if the growing of forage crops is the most important use of the area.

question: What is land tenure and what types are recognised in the WCA 2030?
answer: Land tenure refers to the arrangements or rights under which the holder operates the land. The WCA 2030 distinguishes between legal and non-legal ownership, rented land, and other types such as common land, land provided free, and land under shifting cultivation.
excerpt: Item 0204 refers to the breakdown of the total area of the holding according to specific land tenure types. A holding may have one or more tenure types corresponding to each land parcel. Land tenure refers to the arrangements or rights under which the holder operates the land making up the holding. A distinction is made between legal and non-legal ownership, as this is a key element of tenure security.

question: What is the definition of irrigation in the WCA 2030?
answer: Irrigation refers to the intentional supply of water to land, as opposed to naturally occurring rainfall. It includes any process by which water is transported from a source such as rivers, wells or ponds to agricultural land.
excerpt: Irrigation refers to the intentional supply of water to land, as opposed to the supply by natural precipitation. Irrigation includes any process by which water is transported from a source (rivers, streams, wells or ponds) using equipment such as pumps or manual methods such as buckets.

question: What is the difference between fully controlled and partially controlled irrigation?
answer: Fully controlled irrigation refers to surface, sprinkler and localized irrigation methods. Partially controlled irrigation refers to the use of floodwaters (spate irrigation) or to equipped lowlands, including flood recession cultivation.
excerpt: Irrigation includes fully controlled and partially controlled irrigation. Fully controlled irrigation refers to surface, sprinkler and localized irrigation methods. Partially controlled irrigation refers to the use of floodwaters for crop irrigation (spate irrigation) or to equipped lowlands, including water control methods in wetland areas and inland valley bottoms, as well as flood recession cultivation.

question: Which irrigation item is essential and which are additional?
answer: Item 0302 (Area of land actually irrigated: fully controlled and partially controlled irrigation) is the only essential irrigation item. Items 0301 and 0303–0310 are all additional, covering irrigation use, methods, sources, payment terms, area equipped, and drainage.
excerpt: Essential items: For the holdings — 0302 Area of land actually irrigated: fully controlled and partially controlled irrigation. Additional items (see Theme 3 in Annex 4): 0301 Use of irrigation on the holding: fully and partially controlled irrigation; 0303 Area of land actually irrigated according to land use type; 0304 Area of land actually irrigated according to method of irrigation; 0305 Area of crops actually irrigated for each crop type; 0306 Sources of irrigation water; 0307 Payment terms for irrigation water; 0308 Use of other types of irrigation; 0309 Area equipped for irrigation in working order; 0310 Presence of drainage equipment.

question: What is the difference between area of temporary crops planted and area harvested?
answer: Area planted (Item 0402+) refers to the total physical area on which a temporary crop is established, measured at the time of planting. Area harvested (Item 0403) refers to the total area from which a crop is gathered, excluding areas where harvest was lost due to drought, flood, pests or other causes.
excerpt: Area harvested refers to the total area from which a crop is gathered. Area where harvest was lost because of drought, flooding, pest attack or other causes is excluded. A percentage loss criterion, such as the proportion of area lost due to extreme events (droughts, floods, etc.), may be applied to determine whether a crop should be considered destroyed. Crops that are damaged but not destroyed are included in the area harvested.

question: How are intercropped and mixed crops measured in the agricultural census?
answer: For intercropping, the total area of each crop is estimated by apportioning the plot in proportion to the area each crop occupies, and the sum of all crop areas must equal the total plot area. For mixed crops, estimates based on seed quantity, plant density or eye estimates are used.
excerpt: For intercropping, the plot or field is assigned to each crop in proportion to the area it occupies, and the sum of the individual crop areas must equal the total area of the plot or field. The same applies to mixed crops, where more than one crop, often several, is grown unsystematically in a plot or field. In such cases, it is more difficult to calculate the areas and some estimation is required.

question: What is the Indicative Crop Classification and how does it relate to CPC codes?
answer: The Indicative Crop Classification (ICC) in Annex 6 is the recommended crop classification for the agricultural census, identifying whether crops are temporary or permanent. In the WCA 2030, the ICC has been updated to be consistent with the Central Product Classification (CPC) Version 3.0.
section_title: Statistical unit, concepts, content, and classifications in the WCA 2030
excerpt: The Indicative Crop Classification (ICC) and Classification of Livestock have been updated to be consistent with the Central Product Classification (CPC) Version 3.0 (UN, 2026b) (see Chapter 7, Themes 4 and 5).

question: What fertilizing products are covered in the WCA 2030?
answer: Item 0413 covers mineral fertilizers, organic fertilizers, organo-mineral fertilizers, biofertilizers, animal manure, other organic materials to enhance plant growth, and biostimulants.
excerpt: For the purposes of the agricultural census, fertilizers are mineral or organic substances applied to soils or plants to provide nutrients and improve plant growth. Mineral fertilizers are nutrient-rich products prepared from inorganic materials. Organic fertilizers are carbon-rich products prepared from processed plant or animal materials. Biofertilizers are products containing living or dormant micro-organisms. Animal manure is material from livestock production operations used for fertilization purposes.

question: What is included in the definition of livestock for the agricultural census?
answer: Livestock refers to all animals, birds and insects kept or reared in captivity for agricultural purposes, including cattle, buffaloes, equines, camels, sheep, goats, pigs, avian species (poultry), bees (counted by number of colonies), silkworms and others as listed in Annex 8.
excerpt: The number of animals refers to the animal population on the holding at a specific point in time, usually the census reference day. The animal population is the number of animals being raised by the holding on the reference date, regardless of ownership. Species include cattle, buffaloes, horses and other equine animals, camels, sheep, goats and pigs, as well as avian species (poultry), bees (counted by number of colonies), silkworms and others (see Annex 8).

question: What is a livestock production system in the WCA 2030?
answer: A livestock production system (LPS) describes the general characteristics and practices of raising livestock on the holding. The WCA 2030 identifies integrated crop-livestock (mixed) systems and specialized livestock systems (landless, and range-based or extensive systems).
excerpt: The livestock production system (LPS) refers to the general characteristics and practices of raising livestock on the holding. There are large variations in the scale and intensity of livestock production systems and it is difficult for a classification system to capture all of this diversity. For the purpose of the agricultural census the following livestock systems are identified: 1. Integrated crop-livestock production systems or "mixed" LPS; 2. Specialized livestock production systems.

question: What is the difference between number of animals and number of female breeding animals?
answer: Number of animals (Item 0502) is the total count of each livestock species on the holding at the census reference day. Number of female breeding animals (Item 0503) is the count of female animals kept specifically for reproduction of the species.
excerpt: The number of animals refers to the animal population on the holding at a specific point in time, usually the census reference day. The animal population is the number of animals being raised by the holding on the reference date, regardless of ownership. Number of female breeding animals (Item 0503): breeding animals refers to the number of female animals that are kept for the production of offspring.

question: What types of pesticides are covered in the WCA 2030?
answer: Pesticides covered include insecticides (kill or repel insects), herbicides (destroy or inhibit plant growth), fungicides (destroy or inhibit fungi), plant growth regulators, and rodenticides (kill or repel rodents).
excerpt: Pesticides refer to any substance, or mixture of substances of chemical, biological or genetic (synthetic) ingredients intended for repelling, destroying or controlling any pest, or regulating plant growth. Insecticides are substances used to kill or repel insects. Herbicides are substances used to destroy or inhibit the growth of plants, such as weeds. Fungicides are substances that destroy or inhibit the growth of fungi. Rodenticides are substances that kill, repel or control rodents.

question: What machinery and equipment items are essential in the agricultural census?
answer: Item 0604 (Selected machinery and equipment used on the holding by source) is the only essential machinery item in Theme 6. It covers all machinery, equipment and implements used as inputs to agricultural production, from simple hand tools to complex machinery like combine harvesters.
excerpt: This item identifies machinery and equipment used on the holding, wholly or partly for agricultural production. Machinery and equipment used exclusively for purposes other than agricultural production should be excluded. A broad concept of machinery and equipment is used for the agricultural census, covering all machinery, equipment and implements used as inputs to agricultural production. This ranges from simple hand tools, such as a hoe, to complex machinery, such as a combine harvester.

question: What items are included under Theme 7 (Services for Agriculture) in the WCA 2030?
answer: All eight items in Theme 7 are additional: receipt of credit (0701), source of credit (0702), type of collateral (0703), period of loan (0704), sources of agricultural information (0705), sources of extension services (0706), travelling time to nearest agricultural market (0707), and type of insurance coverage (0708+).
excerpt: THEME 7: SERVICES FOR AGRICULTURE — Additional items (see Theme 7 in Annex 4) For the holdings: 0701 Receipt of credit for agricultural purposes; 0702 Source of credit; 0703 Type of collateral for credit; 0704 Period of loan or credit; 0705 Sources of agricultural information; 0706 Sources of agricultural extension services used; 0707 Travelling time to nearest periodic or permanent agricultural produce market for selling products; 0708+ Type of insurance coverage.

question: What items are included under Theme 8 (Demographic and Social Characteristics)?
answer: All five items in Theme 8 are additional and collected for each household member: sex (0801), age (0802), relationship to household head (0803), marital status (0804), and educational attainment for each member excluding the holder and spouse (0805).
excerpt: THEME 8: DEMOGRAPHIC AND SOCIAL CHARACTERISTICS — Additional items (see Theme 8 in Annex 4) For each household member: 0801 Sex; 0802 Age; 0803 Relationship to household head or other reference person; 0804 Marital status; 0805 Educational attainment for each household member excluding holder and spouse.

question: Are there essential items in Theme 7 or Theme 8?
answer: No. All items in Theme 7 (Services for Agriculture) and Theme 8 (Demographic and Social Characteristics) are classified as additional items. Neither theme contains any essential items.
excerpt: THEME 7: SERVICES FOR AGRICULTURE — All items of this theme are classified as additional; see Annex 4. THEME 8: DEMOGRAPHIC AND SOCIAL CHARACTERISTICS — All items of this theme are classified as additional; see Annex 4.

question: What types of work on the holding are covered in Theme 9?
answer: Theme 9 covers work provided by household members (essential Items 0901 and 0902) and work by external workers including paid employees (Item 0903) and agricultural service contractors (Item 0905). It does not include domestic and personal services for the household.
excerpt: This theme covers items related to the two types of labour inputs on agricultural holdings: (i) work provided by household or family members, and (ii) work provided by external workers: paid employees and contractors. It does not include domestic and personal services provided for the household's own consumption.

question: Who is considered an employee on the holding?
answer: An employee on the holding is a person who had a job on the holding at some time during the reference year with employee status. This includes regular, seasonal, short-term and casual workers. Household members are excluded from Item 0903 because their work inputs are covered under Item 0902.
excerpt: An employee on the holding is a person who had a job on the holding at some time during the reference year, whose status in employment for that job was "employee". This includes regular employees as well as seasonal, short-term and casual workers. Employees are usually paid in cash or in the form of food or other farm produce. Household members are excluded from Item 0903 because their work inputs to the holding are covered under Item 0902.

question: What is the recommended reference period for collecting work data in the agricultural census?
answer: The reference period for all Theme 9 items is the census reference year (12 months). A 12-month period is recommended over the shorter reference periods used in labour force surveys because it captures the seasonal nature of agricultural work throughout the year.
excerpt: The labour statistics framework uses short reference periods, such as one week or one month, for the operational definitions of forms of work. However, for the agricultural census, a longer observation period, such as 12 months, is more appropriate to capture work inputs into agricultural production throughout the year, given the seasonal nature of many agricultural activities. For all items in this theme, the reference period is the census reference year.

question: What is an agricultural service contractor?
answer: An agricultural service contractor provides specific agricultural services to a holding for an agreed fee under a contractual arrangement. A distinction is made between a contractor and an employee: the contractor is engaged for a fee to provide a service, not hired for a defined remuneration to work on the holding.
excerpt: A distinction is made between hiring an employee to work on the holding for a defined remuneration and engaging a contractor to provide certain agricultural services for an agreed fee. Item 0903 covers only employees. Contract work is covered in Item 0905. Countries may wish to differentiate contractors according to the type of service provided by them, such as crop protection, tree pruning, crop harvesting, sheep shearing or farm administration.

question: What is aquaculture as defined in the WCA 2030?
answer: Aquaculture is the farming of aquatic organisms such as fish, molluscs, crustaceans, plants, crocodiles, alligators and amphibians, implying some form of intervention in the rearing process to enhance production.
excerpt: Aquaculture is the farming of aquatic organisms such as fish, molluscs, crustaceans, plants, crocodiles, alligators and amphibians. Farming implies some form of intervention in the rearing process to enhance production, such as regular stocking, feeding, protection from predators, etc.

question: How is aquaculture distinguished from capture fishing in the WCA 2030?
answer: Aquaculture involves farming under controlled conditions with intervention to enhance production. Capture fishing (ISIC Group 031) involves catching wild aquatic organisms and falls outside the scope of the agricultural census, unless a fishing module is included as additional items.
excerpt: Aquaculture is the farming of aquatic organisms such as fish, molluscs, crustaceans, plants, crocodiles, alligators and amphibians. Farming implies some form of intervention in the rearing process to enhance production, such as regular stocking, feeding, protection from predators, etc. A distinction must be made between aquaculture and other forms of aquatic harvesting. The boundary between aquaculture and capture fishing may be blurred.

question: What is the essential item for aquaculture in the WCA 2030?
answer: Item 1001 (Presence of aquaculture on the holding) is the only essential item in Theme 10. It is used to establish a sampling frame for aquaculture-related supplementary modules and ongoing surveys. Its reference period is the census reference year.
excerpt: 1001 Presence of aquaculture on the holding — Essential item. Reference period: census reference year. In the context of the agricultural census, presence of aquaculture refers to aquacultural production activities carried out in association with agricultural production. This item could be used to establish sampling frames for aquaculture-related census supplementary modules and ongoing surveys on aquaculture.

question: Are forestry and fishing items essential or additional in the WCA 2030?
answer: All forestry items (Items 1101–1105 in Theme 11) and all fishing items (Items 1201–1207 in Theme 12) are classified as additional. Neither theme contains essential items.
excerpt: THEME 11: FORESTRY — Additional items (see Theme 11 in Annex 4): 1101 Presence of woodland on the holding; 1102 Area of woodland; 1103 Purposes of woodland; 1104+ Whether forestry activities are practised; 1105 Whether agroforestry is practised. THEME 12: FISHING — Additional items (see Theme 12 in Annex 4): 1201 Engagement of household members in fishing activities; 1202 Number of household members engaged in fishing activity by sex; 1203 Number of fishers employed by the household by sex.

question: What is community-level data in the agricultural census?
answer: Community-level data is information collected at the community level rather than at the individual holding level. It covers geography, socioeconomic conditions, community infrastructure and services, and development programmes. This component was introduced in the WCA 2010.
excerpt: The collection of community-level data was first introduced in the WCA 2010 programme and is maintained in the current programme. The main focus of an agricultural census is structural data collected at the holding-level. However, some types of community-level data are of interest for policymaking, particularly for planning targeted development programmes.

question: Why is community-level data collected in the agricultural census?
answer: Community-level data is collected because data on rural infrastructure, services and communal resources are more practically gathered at the community level and can complement holding-level data for decentralised planning and rural development policy.
excerpt: Some types of community-level data are of interest for policymaking, particularly for planning targeted development programmes. For example, data on the infrastructure and services available to holdings are useful for designing policies aimed at improving rural infrastructure and services, while data on whether the community is prone to natural disasters or subject to seasonal food shortages can inform food security analysis.

question: How is a community defined as a statistical unit for the agricultural census?
answer: A community is defined as a self-contained unit of social and economic activities. For statistical purposes, it may correspond to the lowest administrative unit, a locality, or another operational unit depending on national circumstances.
excerpt: A community can be defined as a self-contained unit of social and economic activities. Population and housing censuses use the similar concept of locality, which is "a distinct population cluster (also designated as inhabited place, populated centre, settlement and so forth) in which the inhabitants live in neighbouring or contiguous sets of living quarters and that has a name or a locally recognized status". Under these definitions, the community or locality may not be the same as the lowest administrative unit.

question: What categories of community-level items are recommended in the WCA 2030?
answer: The recommended community-level items fall into four categories: (1) Geography; (2) Socioeconomic conditions; (3) Community infrastructure and services; and (4) Development programmes. These are complementary to holding-level data.
excerpt: The WCA 2030 recommends the following items: Geography — 2101 Location; 2102 Agroecological, climatic, topographical or soil types; 2103 Land use; 2104 Area of communal grazing land; 2105 Area of communal forest; 2106 Communal area under water used for aquaculture. Socioeconomic conditions — 2201 Population according to population group; 2202 Number of households; 2203 Economic status; 2204 Main economic activities; 2205 Whether there are seasonal food shortages. Community infrastructure and services (Items 2301–2319). Development programmes — 2401 Presence of specific development projects.

question: What are the main classification variables used for tabulations in the WCA 2030?
answer: Nine main classification variables are recommended for tabulating essential items: (1) geographic division, (2) size of holding by total area, (3) size by crop area or livestock numbers, (4) legal status of holder, (5) sex of holder, (6) age of holder, (7) main purpose of production, (8) type of livestock production system, and (9) land tenure type.
excerpt: 9.12 Nine main classification variables have been identified for tabulations of the essential items in the census reports. These variables are discussed in the following paragraphs. 9.13 Administrative unit or agroecological zone (from Item 0101). 9.14 Legal status of agricultural holder (type of holder) (Item 0103). 9.15 Total area of holding (Item 0201). 9.17 Number of livestock (for a particular livestock species) (Items 0502a–0502f). 9.18 Main purpose of production of the holding (Item 0110). 9.19 Household size by sex and age groups (Item 0106). 9.20 Sex of agricultural holder (Item 0104).

question: What is data disclosure control and why is it needed in census tabulations?
answer: Data disclosure control prevents the release of information that could identify individual holdings. It is applied when a cell in a tabulation contains data from so few holdings that the information could be traced back to a specific respondent, breaching confidentiality.
excerpt: Data collected during the agricultural census must be tabulated and disseminated in a manner that protects the privacy and confidentiality of respondents. To prevent disclosure of confidential information, countries should apply appropriate disclosure avoidance procedures to census data prior to release. Given that all data items collected in the census should be tabulated in at least one basic table, the issue of disclosure applies to every item.

question: What are the recommended modes of disseminating agricultural census results?
answer: Recommended dissemination products include standard reports of main results, summary reports, tables, census maps, atlases, web-based interactive tools, and social media publications. A national seminar and regional seminars are strongly recommended to place results on the national agenda.
excerpt: A census is not complete until the information collected is made available to potential users in a form suited to their needs. Common dissemination products are a final report on the main results and a summary report of key findings. Value added products can also be provided, such as census maps, preferably in digital form. The use of electronic formats also facilitates further analysis by users and is therefore recommended wherever possible.

question: What is safe access to census microdata?
answer: Safe access to microdata means providing researchers access to individual-level census data while protecting respondent confidentiality. Methods include public use files (with anonymisation), licensed or restricted-use files, and mediated access or custom tabulations managed by the data producer.
excerpt: Providing access to microdata requires balancing the demands of the research community with the legislated obligation to maintain the confidentiality of information collected from respondents. To ensure safe access to microdata, both respondents' privacy and data producers' confidentiality requirements must be respected. Different methods of access to microdata are possible, offering different tradeoffs between the level of detail released and the protection of confidentiality.

question: What causes data conflicts between census results and current agricultural statistics?
answer: Data conflicts arise from differences in concepts, definitions, classifications or reference periods between the census and current statistics; coverage differences; sampling errors in surveys; and non-sampling errors in both sources.
excerpt: Conflicts and discrepancies between census data and current agricultural statistics can be due to differences in concepts, definitions, classifications, coverage and reference periods. Before applying any reconciliation method, it is important to decide whether the census data should have priority over data from other sources (the usual case because the census is undertaken without sampling error).

question: What is data archiving in the context of an agricultural census?
answer: Data archiving ensures the long-term preservation of census data, including raw data, questionnaires, documentation and tabulations, to assist users in understanding the data and to maintain a historical record for future research and comparison.
excerpt: Data archiving is a means of ensuring long-term preservation of data and assists users in understanding what data are available, how they can be accessed, and how they should be interpreted and used. Data archiving involves explicitly identifying the census data to be preserved, safe storage of the data, and appropriate documentation to ensure the data can be understood and used in the future.

question: How does the agricultural holding relate to the System of National Accounts?
answer: Under the SNA, the agricultural holding corresponds to an establishment — an economic unit engaged in a single main production activity at a single location. The holding's land parcels in different locations require flexible application of the single-location criterion, allowed under SNA.
section_title: Annex 1: The agricultural census within the framework of the System of National Accounts
excerpt: An establishment is an enterprise or part of an enterprise situated in a single location and primarily engaged in a single type of production activity. An agricultural holding is an economic unit of agricultural production under single management. If the principal economic production activity of the agricultural holding is agricultural production, the holding corresponds to an agricultural establishment under the SNA/ISIC framework.

question: What ISIC categories are covered by the agricultural census scope?
answer: The agricultural census scope covers ISIC Groups 011 (growing of non-perennial crops), 012 (growing of perennial crops), 013 (plant propagation), 014 (animal production), and 015 (mixed farming). ISIC Group 031 (capture fishing) falls outside the scope.
section_title: Annex 2: ISIC scope of the agricultural census
excerpt: ISIC Group 011: Growing of non-perennial crops — This group comprises the activity of growing non-perennial crops – i.e. plants that do not last more than two growing seasons. ISIC Group 012: Growing of perennial crops — This group includes the growing of perennial crops – i.e. plants that last for more than two growing seasons.

question: What ISIC categories cover aquaculture?
answer: ISIC Group 032 covers aquaculture. It consists of two classes: ISIC Class 0321 (Marine aquaculture) and ISIC Class 0322 (Freshwater aquaculture). This annex applies when an aquaculture module is included in the agricultural census or when both censuses are conducted jointly.
excerpt: ISIC Group 032: Aquaculture — This group includes aquaculture (or aquafarming) – i.e. the production process involving the culturing or farming (including harvesting) of aquatic organisms (fish, molluscs, crustaceans, plants, crocodiles, alligators and amphibians) using techniques designed to increase the production of the organisms in question beyond the natural capacity of the environment. It consists of the following ISIC classes: ISIC Class 0321: Marine aquaculture; ISIC Class 0322: Freshwater aquaculture.

question: Which SDG indicators can be partially monitored through agricultural censuses?
answer: The agricultural census can partially provide data for SDG indicator 5.a.1 (women's land ownership and tenure rights), indicator 2.3.1 (volume of production per labour unit by farm size), and indicator 2.3.2 (average income of small-scale food producers by sex and indigenous status).
section_title: Annex 11: SDG indicators covered partially by agricultural censuses
excerpt: In the absence of periodic household surveys, a sample-based module in the agricultural census may provide partial data or coverage for Indicators 5.a.1, 2.3.1, and 2.3.2. Indicator 5.a.1: (a) Proportion of total agricultural population with ownership or secure rights over agricultural land, by sex; and (b) share of women among owners or rights-bearers of agricultural land, by type of tenure. Indicator 2.3.1: Volume of production per labour unit by classes of farming/pastoral/forestry enterprise size. Indicator 2.3.2: Average income of small-scale food producers, by sex and Indigenous status.

question: What ILO frameworks underpin the work-on-the-holding items in the WCA 2030?
answer: Theme 9 is aligned with the Resolution concerning statistics of work, employment and labour underutilization adopted by the 19th ICLS in 2013 and amended by the 21st ICLS in 2023. The WCA 2030 applies a 12-month reference period rather than the shorter periods used in labour force surveys to capture seasonal agricultural work.
section_title: Annex 10: Overview of ILO Resolutions concerning statistics of work
excerpt: The Resolution concerning statistics of work, employment and labour underutilization (ILO, 2013) adopted by the 19th International Conference of Labour Statisticians (ICLS) in 2013 and amended by the 21st ICLS in 2023 defines the basic concept of work as comprising "any activity performed by persons of any sex and age to produce goods or to provide services for use by others or for own-use". Data collection on work on the holding in the agricultural census should be in line with recommendations provided in this Resolution.

question: How do the WCA 2030 land use classes correspond to SEEA and FAO land use classifications?
answer: The relationship between the nine basic WCA 2030 land use classes and the SEEA land use classification categories is provided in Annex 5. The FAO land use classification is the basis for the WCA 2030 classes, which are designed to cover the whole territory of a country.
excerpt: The internationally accepted standard for land use is the FAO land use classification. The relationship between the basic land use classes recommended by the WCA 2030 and the relevant categories or classes of the SEEA land use classification is provided in Annex 5.

question: What is the recommended age reference for the agricultural holder?
answer: The age of the agricultural holder refers to the interval between the date of birth and the date of the census reference day (i.e. completed years of age at the reference day). Age data help study relationships between the age of holders, land area and agricultural practices.
excerpt: The age of the holder is important for studying the relationship between age and farm size, agricultural practices, and rural development. Age refers to the interval between the date of birth and the date of the census reference day. Countries should tabulate data by age groups that are consistent with national conventions and international standards.

question: What other economic activities of the household are collected in Theme 1?
answer: Item 0111 collects information on other economic activities of the household besides own-account agricultural production, using ISIC Rev. 5 categories. This includes activities such as agrotourism, processing of agricultural products, and other economic production activities.
excerpt: Other economic activities are those undertaken by the household, linked to the agricultural holding or not. These may include fishing, collecting forestry products, craft activities or operating a family business. The activity categories listed above are based on ISIC (Rev. 5) codes, with the exception of agrotourism. Agrotourism refers to all tourism-related activities that make use of the land, buildings or other resources of the holding, including accommodation services, farm visits for tourists or other groups, and sport or recreation activities.

question: How are livestock classified in the WCA 2030?
answer: Livestock are classified in Annex 8 into 8 groups: (1) Bovine animals (cattle, buffaloes); (2) Sheep and goats; (3) Swine/pigs; (4) Equines (horses, asses, mules); (5) Camels and camelids; (6) Poultry (chickens, turkeys, geese, ducks, guinea fowls); (7) Other animals; and (8) Insects and worms (bees, silkworms). Each group links to CPC Version 3.0 codes.
excerpt: ANNEX 8 — CLASSIFICATION OF LIVESTOCK: Group 1: Bovine animals (Cattle, Buffaloes, Other bovine animals). Group 2: Sheep and goats. Group 3: Swine/pigs. Group 4: Equines (Horses, Asses, Mules and hinnies). Group 5: Camels and camelids. Group 6: Poultry (Chickens, Turkeys, Geese, Ducks, Guinea fowls). Group 7: Other animals (Deer, Rabbits, Fur-bearing animals, Ostriches, Other birds, Other animals). Group 8: Insects and worms (Bees, Silkworms, Other insects and worms).

question: How is machinery and equipment classified in the WCA 2030?
answer: Annex 9 classifies machinery and equipment into three main groups: (1) Manually-operated equipment; (2) Animal-powered equipment; and (3) Machine-powered equipment (further subdivided into general farm machinery, tractors/vehicles, crop machinery, post-harvest machinery, livestock machinery, aquacultural and forestry machinery, and drones/robots). Each entry links to HS 2022 codes.
section_title: Annex 9: Classification of machinery and equipment
excerpt: ANNEX 9 — CLASSIFICATION OF MACHINERY AND EQUIPMENT: Group 1: Manually-operated equipment. Group 2: Animal-powered equipment. Group 3: Machine-powered equipment — including: 31 Machines for general farm use; 32 Tractors, bulldozers and other vehicles; 33 Crop machinery and equipment (331 Land preparation and planting; 332 Crop maintenance; 333 Crop harvesting; 334 Post-harvest); 34 Livestock machinery and equipment; 35 Aquacultural machinery and equipment; 36 Forestry machinery and equipment; 37 Unmanned Aerial vehicle and robots.

question: Why is holding-level data important in a census of agriculture?
answer: Holding-level data allow statistics to be tabulated for any defined administrative or geographic area or type of holding, support planning of sample surveys, provide a basis for national accounts, and enable analysis of rare events and small-area estimation.
excerpt: The census of agriculture involves the collection of data at the individual holding-level. It provides fundamental data on the organizational structure of agricultural holdings, such as the number of farms and farm size, land use, land tenure, livestock. Holding-level census data collected for every holding in the target population allow statistics at the level of the lowest administrative area. This often means that data are not available at the desired administrative levels across the country. Holding-level data from the agricultural census support proper planning and implementation of sample surveys.

question: What types of frames can be used for the household sector in an agricultural census?
answer: Frames for the household sector may be built from: a population and housing census EA frame (by identifying agricultural households through screening items); a farm register; or a listing exercise conducted specifically for the agricultural census. Often a combination of frames is used.
excerpt: Another type of frame for the household sector of an agricultural census can be created from the population and housing census as a one-time exercise. Even a list of all households from the population and housing census can provide a useful frame for an agricultural census. Typically, a combination of frames is used for the agricultural census. Often, the household sector is enumerated based on the EA frame of the population and housing census, whereas a frame of agricultural holdings in the non-household sector is obtained from administrative sources.

question: Why is it important for countries to specify the census coverage clearly?
answer: Countries must clearly specify their coverage because any exclusions from the agricultural census affect not only census results but also all subsequent surveys based on the census frame. Users need this information to correctly interpret and analyse the results.
section_title: Coverage of the census of agriculture
excerpt: It should be recognized that, in an integrated agricultural statistical system, any exclusions from the agricultural census do not just affect the results of the agricultural census, but also the surveys that are conducted based on the frame provided by the agricultural census. It is very important that countries clearly specify the coverage in the presentation of agricultural census results. When certain geographic areas are excluded or certain holding types are omitted, this should be highlighted in the census dissemination products to help users interpret and analyse the results.

question: How does the agricultural census serve as a benchmark for current agricultural statistics?
answer: The census provides the most reliable data on crop areas, livestock numbers and other structural variables at every administrative level for the census reference year. These data can be used to establish benchmarks and correction factors for current statistics collected between censuses.
excerpt: A decennial agricultural census cannot be used as a source of current agricultural statistics because it does not provide data frequently enough. However, the agricultural census can provide a reliable benchmark for improving current crop and livestock statistics. For crops, the agricultural census usually provides the most reliable data available on the area of each crop at each administrative level for the census reference year. This is especially the case for minor crops, for which the current statistics are often lacking.

question: What is the role of the agricultural census for rare events and rare crops?
answer: Complete enumeration in the agricultural census ensures coverage of rare crops and livestock species that would be missed in sample surveys. The frame built from the census enables targeted surveys for such rare events in subsequent inter-censal surveys.
excerpt: The most common sampling frames used in agricultural surveys are lists of holdings (which may be household-based). In addition, the census, through complete enumeration, provides a frame for rare crops or livestock species. Rare events should not be confused with non-significant items, which are also rare, but are of no interest to users.

question: What is the AGRISurvey and how does it relate to the agricultural census?
answer: The AGRISurvey is a modular survey programme conducted annually during the inter-censal period, consisting of one core module and three rotating thematic modules. It uses the agricultural census as its sampling frame and is recommended for collecting non-structural items that are not suitable for the census.
section_title: Global Strategy to Improve Agricultural and Rural Statistics
excerpt: An important methodological development under pillar 2 was the elaboration of an Agriculture Integrated Survey (AGRISurvey) methodology for collection of relevant agricultural and rural data on a regular basis. AGRISurvey is a modular survey programme conducted on an annual basis during the inter-censal period. It consists of one core module and three rotating modules: "Farm income, labour and productivity", "Production methods and environment", and "Machinery, equipment and assets".

question: How can objective measurement improve crop area data in the agricultural census?
answer: Objective measurement — using GPS devices or remote sensing — can be employed in sample surveys alongside holder declarations to compute correction factors that improve the accuracy of declared crop areas in the census.
excerpt: The most common method for collecting data on parcel and total holding areas in the census of agriculture is by holder declaration. In some countries, holders may not be able to provide reliable estimates of crop areas. To address this problem, countries with additional resources may employ alternative methods in sample surveys, such as objective measurement in which the area is measured using GPS devices, including involving remote sensing. The accuracy of crop areas could be improved if both declared areas and objective measurements could be drawn from a sample.

question: What is the MSF (Master Sampling Frame) and how does the agricultural census contribute to it?
answer: The MSF is a general-purpose sampling frame created from a population and/or agricultural census, for use in selecting samples for different surveys. The agricultural census builds and updates the MSF so that surveys can draw samples without constructing ad hoc frames each time.
excerpt: The Global Strategy to Improve Agricultural and Rural Statistics advocates for the building of an MSF, which is a general-purpose sampling frame created from a population and housing census and/or an agricultural census, for use in selecting samples for different surveys or different rounds of a periodic survey. The frame is usually maintained by the national statistical office and is updated on an ongoing basis so that it is available for any survey carried out at any time.
